content.text: The Four Azure Scope Levels

Every Azure resource, RBAC assignment, and policy assignment exists at a specific scope. Scope is the boundary within which a setting applies and from which child scopes can inherit it. Azure defines four nested scope levels: management group, subscription, resource group, and resource — each a more specific subset of the one above it.

Understanding scope is foundational to Azure governance because RBAC and Azure Policy both use the scope hierarchy to determine what applies where. An assignment at a broader scope cascades down to all narrower scopes beneath it; an assignment at a narrower scope applies only within that boundary.

attrs.alt: Azure scope hierarchy: management group, subscription, resource group, resource

content.text: Scope Level 1: Management Group

The management group is the broadest governance scope in Azure. It does not contain resources directly — only subscriptions (or other management groups). Its purpose is to provide a single point at which policies and RBAC assignments can be applied to multiple subscriptions simultaneously.
The ARM scope string for a management group is:
/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/{managementGroupId}
The root management group's ID is the Azure AD tenant ID. Every subscription must be in exactly one management group at any time.

# Assign a policy at management group scope
MG_SCOPE='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp'

az policy assignment create \
  --name 'corp-require-tags' \
  --policy '1e30110a-5ceb-460c-a204-c1c3969c6d62' \
  --scope $MG_SCOPE \
  --params '{"tagName":{"value":"environment"}}'

# This policy now applies to every subscription under mg-corp
# and to every resource group and resource within those subscriptions

# Assign Reader RBAC at management group scope
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 \
  --role Reader \
  --scope $MG_SCOPE

# The principal now has Reader on every subscription, resource group,
# and resource beneath mg-corp — via inheritance, not explicit assignment
Scope Level 2: Subscription

The subscription is the billing boundary and the primary unit of scale isolation in Azure. RBAC and policy assignments at subscription scope apply to all resource groups and resources within the subscription, but not to other subscriptions in the same management group.
The ARM scope string for a subscription is:
/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}
Key properties of the subscription scope that differ from management group scope:
Subscriptions carry quota limits — vCPU counts, public IP addresses, VNets — that are tracked at this scope
Billing is aggregated per subscription; cost management budgets and alerts are set at this scope
Subscriptions are the boundary for Azure AD role definitions and custom role definitions (custom roles are scoped to a subscription or a management group above it)
The subscription Activity Log is a per-subscription resource; aggregation requires a central workspace

# Assign Contributor to a service principal at subscription scope
# This gives the SP access to create resources in any RG in this sub
SUB_SCOPE='/subscriptions/11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111'

az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002 \
  --role Contributor \
  --scope $SUB_SCOPE

# Check quota usage at subscription scope
az vm list-usage \
  --location eastus \
  --subscription 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 \
  --query '[?currentValue > `0`].{Name:localName, Used:currentValue, Limit:limit}' \
  -o table

# Assign a policy directly at subscription scope
# (overrides or supplements MG-level policy for this subscription only)
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'sub-allowed-vm-skus' \
  --policy '75c4f823-d65c-4f29-a733-01d0077fdbcb' \
  --scope $SUB_SCOPE \
  --params '{"listOfAllowedSKUs":{"value":["Standard_D2s_v5","Standard_D4s_v5"]}}'
Scope Level 3: Resource Group

The resource group is the primary deployment and lifecycle unit. It is a logical container for resources that share the same lifecycle — created together, updated together, deleted together. RBAC and policy assignments at resource group scope apply only to the resources within that resource group.
The ARM scope string for a resource group is:
/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}

Resource group names must be unique within a subscription. They can contain alphanumeric characters, underscores, periods, and hyphens, up to 90 characters. The resource group itself must exist in a specific Azure region, but resources within it can be in any region.

# Create a resource group
az group create \
  --name rg-payments-prod \
  --location eastus \
  --tags environment=production workload=payments

RG_SCOPE='/subscriptions/11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111/resourceGroups/rg-payments-prod'

# Grant Contributor to a team at resource group scope only
# They cannot access other RGs in the same subscription
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000003 \
  --role Contributor \
  --scope $RG_SCOPE

# Assign a policy at resource group scope
# Useful for workload-specific rules that differ across RGs in the same subscription
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'rg-payments-allowed-locations' \
  --policy 'e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4f' \
  --scope $RG_SCOPE \
  --params '{"listOfAllowedLocations":{"value":["eastus","eastus2"]}}'

# Delete an entire resource group (and all resources in it)
# This is why grouping by lifecycle is important
az group delete --name rg-payments-prod --yes --no-wait
Scope Level 4: Resource

The resource scope is the narrowest scope level. RBAC can be assigned at resource scope to give a principal access to a single resource without granting broader access to the resource group or subscription. Azure Policy does not support assignments at resource scope.
The ARM scope string for a resource follows the pattern:
/subscriptions/{subId}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/{resourceProvider}/{resourceType}/{resourceName}
Examples of resource scope strings:

# A storage account
/subscriptions/11111111.../resourceGroups/rg-data/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/stpayments001

# A Key Vault
/subscriptions/11111111.../resourceGroups/rg-security/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/kv-payments-prod

# A specific VM
/subscriptions/11111111.../resourceGroups/rg-compute/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-web-01

# Grant a service principal access to a single Key Vault only
KV_SCOPE=$(az keyvault show \
  --name kv-payments-prod \
  --resource-group rg-security \
  --query id -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000004 \
  --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
  --scope $KV_SCOPE

# The SP can now read secrets from kv-payments-prod
# but has no access to any other resource in the subscription

# Grant a user Blob Data Reader on a single storage account
SA_SCOPE=$(az storage account show \
  --name stpayments001 \
  --resource-group rg-data \
  --query id -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000005 \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
  --scope $SA_SCOPE

Resource-scope RBAC is appropriate for service principals that need access to exactly one resource — a CI pipeline that deploys to one App Service, or an application that reads from one Key Vault. For humans, prefer higher-scope assignments to reduce the number of role assignments (a subscription has a limit of 4,000 role assignments).
How Inheritance Works Across Scope Levels
RBAC and policy assignments inherit downward through the scope hierarchy. An assignment at a parent scope is effective at all child scopes, without any explicit child assignment being required.
Assignment scope
Effective at
Management group
All subscriptions under the MG, all resource groups in those subscriptions, all resources in those resource groups
Subscription
All resource groups in the subscription, all resources in those resource groups
Resource group
All resources within the resource group only
Resource
That single resource only — no inheritance below (resources have no children in the ARM hierarchy)

# Demonstrate inheritance: list effective permissions on a resource
# including permissions inherited from subscription and MG
RESOURCE_ID='/subscriptions/11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111/resourceGroups/rg-payments-prod/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/stpayments001'
USER_OID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001'

# List all role assignments that apply to this resource
# (direct, inherited from RG, subscription, and MG)
az role assignment list \
  --assignee $USER_OID \
  --scope $RESOURCE_ID \
  --include-inherited \
  --include-groups \
  --query '[].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope, Type:type}' \
  -o table

# List effective RBAC permissions (what actions the principal can actually perform)
az role assignment list \
  --assignee $USER_OID \
  --all \
  --include-inherited \
  --query '[?contains(scope, `stpayments001`)].{Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}' \
  -o table
Scope and Azure Policy

Azure Policy is assigned at management group, subscription, or resource group scope — not at resource scope. The policy engine evaluates all policies that apply to a resource by walking up the scope hierarchy and collecting every assignment from resource group to subscription to management group.

# List all policy assignments that apply to a specific resource
# by checking each scope level in turn

SUB='11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111'
RG='rg-payments-prod'

echo '=== Policies at Resource Group scope ==='
az policy assignment list \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/$RG" \
  --query '[].{Name:displayName,Effect:parameters.effect.value}' -o table

echo '=== Policies at Subscription scope ==='
az policy assignment list \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUB" \
  --query '[].{Name:displayName,Effect:parameters.effect.value}' -o table

echo '=== Policies at Corp MG scope (inherited) ==='
az policy assignment list \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp' \
  --query '[].{Name:displayName,Effect:parameters.effect.value}' -o table

# The effective policy set is the union of all three — conflicts resolved by effect strength
# deny > audit > append > deployIfNotExists > auditIfNotExists
The Role Assignment Limit per Scope

Azure enforces a limit of 4,000 role assignments per subscription. This limit counts all assignments within the subscription — including those at management group scope that are inherited. Assigning roles at resource scope multiplies the assignment count rapidly. Best practice: assign at the broadest scope that satisfies the security requirement, and use groups rather than individual users to reduce count.
Scope level
Assignment limit
Counted against subscription limit?
Management group
500 per management group
Yes — each MG assignment is counted against each child subscription's limit
Subscription
4,000 total per subscription
Yes — this is the cap
Resource group
Counted within the subscription 4,000
Yes
Resource
Counted within the subscription 4,000
Yes — narrow-scope assignments deplete the limit fastest

# Check the number of role assignments in a subscription
SUB='11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111'

TOTAL=$(az role assignment list \
  --subscription $SUB \
  --include-inherited \
  --query 'length(@)' -o tsv)

echo "Total role assignments in $SUB: $TOTAL / 4000"

# Break down by scope level
az role assignment list --subscription $SUB --include-inherited \
  --query '[].scope' -o tsv | sed 's|/subscriptions/[^/]*/||' | cut -d/ -f1 | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
Choosing the Right Scope Level
Scenario
Recommended scope
Reason
Platform policy that must apply to all subscriptions (e.g., require tags)
Management group
Single assignment cascades to all child subscriptions automatically
Team owns a subscription and needs full control within it
Subscription
Subscription boundary provides billing and quota isolation
CI/CD pipeline deploys a specific workload
Resource group
Pipeline has access to its resources; cannot affect other workloads in the subscription
Application reads from exactly one Key Vault
Resource (Key Vault)
Least-privilege: no access to anything else in the subscription
Security auditor needs read access across all subscriptions
Management group (Reader)
One assignment provides visibility everywhere without individual subscription grants
Sandbox developer needs to experiment freely in their own space
Subscription (Owner)
Full control within their subscription; policy guardrails applied via MG prevent misuse
Scope in ARM Templates and Bicep

ARM templates and Bicep files are deployed at a specific scope. The scope determines what resources can be created — management group-scoped deployments can create management groups and assign policies at MG level; subscription-scoped deployments can create resource groups; resource group-scoped deployments create resources within that RG.
Resource group-scoped deployment (default)

# Default: deploy a template at resource group scope
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group rg-payments-prod \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @params.json

# Resource group-scoped templates can only create resources
# They cannot create resource groups or subscription-level resources
Subscription-scoped deployment

# Deploy at subscription scope
az deployment sub create \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file main-sub.bicep \
  --parameters location=eastus
Management group-scoped deployment

# Deploy at management group scope
az deployment mg create \
  --management-group-id mg-corp \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file main-mg.bicep

# Tenant-scoped deployment (root MG, creates new MGs from scratch)
az deployment tenant create \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file main-tenant.bicep

The `targetScope` declaration in Bicep and the `$schema` URL in ARM JSON determine what scope the template can address. A resource group template cannot create resource groups; a subscription template cannot create management groups. Pick the broadest scope needed and use `scope:` on modules to push resources to narrower scopes.
Terraform: Targeting Different Scope Levels

Terraform providers for Azure map scope levels to different resource types and data sources. The `azurerm` provider uses different resource types for MG-level, subscription-level, and resource group-level resources.
KQL: Querying Resources by Scope
Azure Resource Graph queries run across all subscriptions the caller can access. The resource IDs returned contain the full scope path, making it straightforward to filter or group by scope level.

// 1. Count resources by resource group across all subscriptions
Resources
| extend resourceGroup = tolower(resourceGroup)
| summarize resourceCount = count() by subscriptionId, resourceGroup
| join kind=leftouter (
    ResourceContainers
    | where type == 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
    | project subscriptionId, subscriptionName = name
) on subscriptionId
| project subscriptionName, resourceGroup, resourceCount
| order by subscriptionName asc, resourceCount desc

// 2. List all RBAC assignments at subscription scope (not inherited from MG)
// Uses the AuthorizationResources type
AuthorizationResources
| where type == 'microsoft.authorization/roleassignments'
| extend scope = tostring(properties.scope)
| where scope matches regex @'^/subscriptions/[^/]+$'
| project subscriptionId, principalId = tostring(properties.principalId),
          roleId = tostring(properties.roleDefinitionId), scope

// 3. Find resource groups with no resources (candidates for cleanup)
ResourceContainers
| where type == 'microsoft.resources/resourcegroups'
| project rgName = name, subscriptionId, resourceGroup
| join kind=leftouter (
    Resources
    | summarize count() by resourceGroup, subscriptionId
) on resourceGroup, subscriptionId
| where isnull(count_)
| project subscriptionId, emptyRG = rgName
Common Scope Mistakes
Mistake
Consequence
Prevention
Assigning roles to individual users at resource scope
4,000 role assignment limit depleted quickly; complex audit trail
Assign to Entra ID groups at the broadest appropriate scope
Deploying subscription-level resources (policy, budgets) in a resource group-scoped template
Template fails — cannot create subscription resources at RG scope
Use targetScope = 'subscription' in Bicep or az deployment sub create
Assigning policy at resource group scope when a subscription-level assignment would suffice
Assignment proliferation; harder to audit effective policy set
Prefer MG or subscription scope unless the rule genuinely varies per RG
Confusing management group scope strings with subscription scope strings
az CLI returns an error; ARM returns 400 InvalidScope
MG scope: /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/{id}  —  not /managementGroups/{id}
Granting Contributor at MG scope to a CI/CD service principal
SP has Contributor on every subscription including production
Grant Contributor at the specific resource group or subscription the pipeline deploys to
Summary

Azure's four scope levels — management group, subscription, resource group, and resource — form a hierarchy where assignments at broader scopes inherit down to narrower ones. Management groups are the right scope for organisation-wide governance policy and cross-subscription RBAC. Subscriptions are the billing and quota boundary. Resource groups are the deployment and lifecycle unit. Resources are the narrowest scope, appropriate for fine-grained RBAC but not policy. The practical rule is: assign at the broadest scope that satisfies least-privilege, use groups rather than individuals, and prefer management group policy inheritance over per-subscription assignments wherever the rule is universal.
